data.title: Lockheed Martin details $105M contract to upgrade GPS ground systems
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) on Thursday provided more details about a contract valued at up to $105 million by the U.S. Space Force to continue work on the Global Positioning System’s ground control infrastructure. The award is part of an ongoing effort to modernize the network that supports positioning, navigation and timing services worldwide.

The agreement extends more than a decade of work tied to the Space Force’s Architecture Evolution Plan, under which Lockheed (LMT) has been updating the GPS ground segment that manages satellites and delivers signals to military and civilian users. The latest contract includes support for launch, early orbit operations and eventual disposal of upcoming GPS IIIF satellites, along with broader system upgrades.

For investors, the award underscores the steady, long-duration nature of U.S. defense space spending, where incremental modernization contracts can provide predictable revenue streams for prime contractors. It also highlights Lockheed’s (LMT) entrenched position in GPS infrastructure, a critical system with high switching costs that can support recurring work tied to upgrades, sustainment and next-generation satellite deployments.

The GPS ground segment plays a central role in ensuring that signals from orbiting satellites remain accurate and resilient, particularly for military operations that depend on secure and jam-resistant navigation. Lockheed said the work will focus in part on improving system resilience as new satellites with enhanced capabilities are introduced.

The company has completed production of its GPS III satellites and is now manufacturing the next-generation GPS IIIF series at its Denver facility. These satellites are designed to deliver stronger anti-jamming signals and improved accuracy compared with earlier generations.

Under the new contract, Lockheed (LMT) will help integrate those satellites into the broader GPS enterprise, including enabling the deployment of M-Code, an encrypted military signal intended to provide greater protection against interference.

The modernization effort reflects a broader push by the Space Force to strengthen the resilience of space-based infrastructure that supports both defense operations and commercial activities such as transportation systems, financial networks and utilities.

data.title: 3 things to look forward to on Friday
data.description:
U.S. stocks closed at new highs on Thursday, a day after the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) had reached records on hopes that the U.S.-Iran war could be over soon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.3%, marking a record close of 7,041.20 points, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) finished +0.4% at the new all-time high close of 24,102.70 points, and the Dow (DJI) closed +0.2%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 3 basis points higher at 4.32%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.79%.

Now, here are three things to focus on Friday:

  • Earnings season continues with several bank companies reporting earnings. Those include Truist Financial Corp. (TFC), Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), Regions Financial Corp. (RF), Ally Financial (ALLY), and BancFirst Corp. (BANF), among others.
  • In the crypto world, BlackRock (BLK) poured $505.7M into its iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) in just 48 hours, amounting to holdings of $59.31B. The cryptocurrency has advanced around 1.9% in the past month to around $75,200 currently.
  • Lastly, with U.S. President Donald Trump hinting that the war is “close to being over,” U.S. stocks have risen to new highs. Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon agreed on a 10-day ceasefire. In addition, the U.S. and Iran are reportedly returning to Pakistan next week for a second round of peace talks, according to two senior Pakistani officials involved in finalizing decisions with both the U.S. and Iranian teams. Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) have declined around 3.4% in the past five days. Investors will continue to monitor oil markets and the Middle East.

data.title: Blaize, NeoTensr enter into contract for up to $50M to deploy AI edge data center infrastructure
data.description:
• Blaize (BZAI) on Thursday announced an up to $50M contract with hardware and software system integrator NeoTensr to co-develop AI edge data center infrastructure, targeting Asia-Pacific’s growing compute demand.
  • The contract is expected to generate up to $50M in revenue within the first year, the companies said.
  • The offering includes co-branded AI servers and full-stack solutions capable of handling 200+ camera streams per server.
  • Blaize Holdings (BZAI) closed at $1.73, up 4.85%, and extended gains in after-hours trading to $2.13, up 23.12% following the announcement.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: DoE rejects CenterPoint Energy's request to close Indiana coal plant
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Energy rejected a request from CenterPoint Energy (CNP) to allow the 60-year-old F.B. Culley coal plant in Indiana to close, Bloomberg reported Thursday, demonstrating the Trump administration's push to keep aging coal plants online that it says are needed to support an electric grid under stress from rising power demand.

CenterPoint (CNP) had asked the DoE not to renew the stay-open mandate for the plant, saying that extending an order halting the planned December 2025 retirement would require millions of dollars in upgrades and lengthy outages to "support an inefficient and increasingly unreliable asset."

The February 17 letter asked the DoE not to renew its December order keeping the plant open, but the agency extended that directive, along with another order for an Indiana coal unit owned by the Northern Indiana Public Service Company, through June 21 in March.

"This letter shows that even utilities like CenterPoint admit that there is no grid emergency and that coal plants are too unreliable, expensive, and polluting to continue operating," Citizens Action Coalition program director Ben Inskeep told Bloomberg.

data.title: Baseball streaming rights see federal antitrust scrutiny - report
data.description:
US regulators are examining the distribution of streaming rights for Major League Baseball as part of a larger federal probe into how professional sports leagues provide their games to online platforms.

Federal Communications Commission Chairman Brendan Carr said that he's listening to baseball fans who are complaining about where to watch MLB games, according to an interview with Bloomberg. 

'One thing that did surprise me was the level of interest of baseball fans in particular,” Carr told Bloomberg.

The probe comes after the WSJ reported last Thursday that the Department of Justice is investigating whether the National Football League’s policy of splitting its broadcast privileges is anticompetitive and harms consumers.

“You could make the argument that there are other sports leagues out there that are potentially pushing the limits of the Sports Broadcasting Act even further than what the NFL has,” Carr said. “The NFL is something that everyone is aware of and focuses on. And so I speak of it just as a shorthand, but we are focused more broadly on other leagues as well.”

The DOJ also intends to examine the streaming rights of other leagues covered under the Sports Broadcasting Act, including MLB, Bloomberg reported, citing a person familiar with the matter. A spokesperson for the DOJ declined to comment.
